retrieved_docs.title: Ricin Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ricin is a lectin (carbohydrate-binding protein) produced in the endosperm of the seeds of the castor oil plant. Ingestion of as few as two castor oil plant seeds has been shown to be toxic, although patients have survived ingesting as many as 30. Ricin is also toxic by inhalation or injection, and as little as five to ten micrograms per kilogram by inhalation can be lethal. Ricin was developed as a biological weapon by both the United States and the Soviet Union. The castor oil plant grows in the wild in tropical climates. Approximately two million tons of castor seeds are produced yearly around the globe. Castor seeds can be crushed to extract castor oil, which has industrial applications in lubricants, dyes, preservatives, and pharmaceuticals. The waste mash of castor seeds after the oil extraction process is approximately 5% ricin by weight. This waste can be detoxified and used as a fertilizer or as a supplement in animal feed. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: High Fiber Diet -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Based on physicochemical properties; Soluble fiber: They are water-soluble and derived from the inner flesh of plants such as pectin, gums, and mucilage. They form a viscous gel and are usually fermented by bacteria in the colon into gases and by-products such as short-chain fatty acids. They alter the blood glucose and cholesterol concentrations. [5] Sources of soluble fibers are: Fruits Oat Barley Legumes, peas, and beans Vegetables such as broccoli and carrots Most root vegetables

retrieved_docs.title: Euphorbia tirucalli Toxicity -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Euphorbia tirucalli ingestions usually only cause mild symptoms, and treatment is supportive. [1] Cold fluids, ice, or ice cream may alleviate irritated oral mucosa, and viscous lidocaine may be considered for more severe cases. It is uncertain whether activated charcoal will help neutralize or prevent absorption of Euphorbia sap. However, it may be reasonable to administer in high plant ingestion cases that present within 1 hour, as long as the patient is otherwise an appropriate candidate for activated charcoal administration. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Carbohydrates -- Introduction -- Types
retrieved_docs.content: Fiber: Non-digestible complex carbohydrates that encourage healthy bacterial growth in the colon and act as a bulking agent, easing defecation. The main components include cellulose, hemicellulose, and pectin. Insoluble: Remains in the intestines, thereby softening and bulking the stool. Benefits include regularity of bowel movements and a decreased risk of diverticulosis. Examples: brans, seeds, vegetables, brown rice, and potato skins. Soluble: Helps decrease blood cholesterol and LDL levels, reduces straining with defecation, and blunts postprandial blood glucose levels. Examples are fleshy fruit, oats, broccoli, and dried beans.

retrieved_docs.title: Biologic, Chemical, and Radiation Terrorism Review -- Issues of Concern -- Biologic Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Ricin is a toxic lectin produced by the castor oil plant and found in the seeds. A dose the size of a few grains of table salt can kill a human. The LD 50 is about 22 micrograms per kilogram of body weight. Injection or inhalation is more toxic than oral ingestion. Signs/Symptoms: Toxic if inhaled, injected, or ingested. It causes abdominal pain, coughing, diarrhea, fever, nausea, necrotizing pneumonia, pulmonary edema, shock, tracheobronchitis, vomiting, and weakness. Treatment: Charcoal lavage and supportive care. Biologic Warfare: Several countries have considered or attempted to weaponized ricin. Given ricin's extreme toxicity it is noteworthy that the production of the toxin is rather difficult to limit. The castor bean plant from which ricin is derived is a common ornamental and can be grown at home without any special care. STAPHYLOCOCCUS AUREUS (Enterotoxin Type B)

Still, the majority of meals were freeze-dried.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 577
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin A is a fat‑soluble retinoid that belongs to the structural class of retinol acetate and functions as an antioxidant, as indicated by its relationship to “antioxidancia.” Enzyme interactions describe a mechanism of action for vitamin A, reflecting its influence on enzymatic pathways. Vitamin A appears in a wide variety of pharmaceutical and nutraceutical products, both as an active moiety/ingredient and, in some topical or supplemental formulations, as an inactive component. The sublingual solution “A11 ENVIRO‑PROTECT” (containing Alternaria alternata, Cladosporium sphaerospermum, Cochliobolus sativus, Cochliobolus spicifer, Aspergillus niger var. niger, Gibberella fujikuroi, Mucor plumbeus, Penicillium chrysogenum var. chrysogenum, Chelidonium majus, mineral oil, Lycopodium clavatum spore, carbon monoxide, chlorine, Malus sylvestris flower, house dust, Berberis vulgaris root bark, cadmium, Aureobasidium pullulans var. pullutans, Rhizopus stolonifer, niacin, lead, Quercus robur

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 341
retrieved_docs.content: Attapulgite and methylated pectin are both classified as members of the “Other intestinal adsorbents in ATC” category, indicating that they belong to the same therapeutic group of intestinal adsorbent agents. Within this group, attapulgite is noted to have a physiologic effect of stimulating fluid and electrolyte absorption in the large intestine, a property that can influence intestinal water balance and may be relevant in the management of conditions such as diarrhea. The inclusion of both attapulgite and methylated pectin in this category highlights their shared role as intestinal adsorbents, while attapulgite’s specific action on large‑intestine fluid and electrolyte uptake underscores its potential therapeutic benefit in supporting normal intestinal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1287
retrieved_docs.content: or ancillary benefits rather than primary pharmacologic effect. From a mechanistic perspective, vitamin A exerts physiological effects classified as “Cellular Activity Alteration,” influencing cell differentiation, growth, and immune function. Its mechanism of action includes specific enzyme interactions that modulate retinoid signaling pathways. Structurally, vitamin A belongs to the retinol acetate class, underscoring its chemical relationship to retinoids. Additionally, vitamin A is related to antioxidant activity, contributing to the neutralization of free radicals and protection of cellular membranes. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that vitamin A serves multiple roles across pharmaceutical and nutraceutical products: as a vital nutrient in multivitamin regimens, as a component of complex botanical and mineral blends, as an excipient in protein‑based or topical formulations, and as a biologically active retinoid with cellular, enzymatic, and antioxidant functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2120
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K is classified as a fat‑soluble vitamin, meaning it belongs to the broader group of vitamins that dissolve in lipids and require dietary fat for optimal absorption. This places vitamin K within the same category as vitamins A, D, and E, highlighting shared characteristics such as storage in fatty tissues and the need for adequate fat intake to ensure proper bioavailability. Recognizing vitamin K as a fat‑soluble vitamin is essential for understanding its absorption dynamics, its role in physiological processes like blood coagulation, and for guiding nutritional recommendations that support its effectiveness.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2290
retrieved_docs.content: Amylasy is classified as a member of the hydrolasy family, indicating that it is a specific type of hydrolasy enzyme. As a subclass of hydrolasy, amylasy shares the general catalytic mechanism of hydrolytic enzymes—using water to cleave chemical bonds—but it is specialized for breaking down starches and other polysaccharides. This hierarchical relationship places amylasy within the broader category of hydrolasy enzymes, highlighting its functional role in carbohydrate digestion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Horse chestnut seed is classified as a member of the group “Other capillary stabilizing agents in ATC,” indicating that it is recognized within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system as a capillary‑stabilizing agent. This categorization highlights the therapeutic role of horse chestnut seed in supporting vascular health by strengthening capillary walls and reducing capillary fragility.
